scenario: lips swelling after eating a sandwich
patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "Hi. Hello."] Hi, hi I'm Maria.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Hello, I'm Doctor Jacob and welcome to Babylon."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>
[Clinician asked: "How are you doing?"] I'm all right, thank you. Well I feel a little weird today, to be honest.
[Clinician asked: "Yeah, so just before we  we start, is it all right if you could confirm your full name for me please?"] Yes. Maria Trabalotsa.
[Clinician asked: "OK, day and your date of birth?"] That is the first of October, ninety-nine.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and your email address for me please?"] Yes, M Traba at Gmail dot com.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK, and just to confirm that you're in a secure location and we can have a confidential conversation?"] Yes I think I am, yeah.

[Clinician asked: "Good, right, you've mentioned in  in, in your patient notes that you're having some  sort of lip swelling, and you after you had a sandwich. Is that correct?"] Yeah that is, well, I don't think, I don't think it was, it it wasn't a sandwich but it was, it was something with prawn so. Basically, I, I sometimes go with my friends to, to this place called <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>. And, we, we regularly have like usually I have like a normal vegetarian soup or something.
[Clinician asked: "Mm-hmm."] And yeah, and, then I wanted to try something new.
[Clinician asked: "Mm-hmm."] Um, so what happened was, I, I ordered a prawn soup it it's it it's called  it's like a soup, I ordered a prawn one this time.
[Clinician asked: "Yeah."] And, yeah and then my feel  like my lips started feeling a little weird just in the in the corner sort of on the left first.
[Clinician asked: "Mm-hmm."] And then, I don't know and then, we went back we had little bit of chat, of banter I wasn't really, paying attention.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] Uh, now I feel the that there is a swelling, there is like a swelling on my, on my upper lip and it's kind of getting bigger. I don't know what to do.
[Clinician asked: "Yeah. Right. All right. So when did you have the prawn soup? What time?"] That was, that must have been just after work, so maybe like around seven.

[Clinician asked: "OK, around seven o'clock, and um how long did it take to develop the swelling?"] Well I wasn't really paying attention so, it must have been like, must have been like, I don't know half an hour maybe.
[Clinician asked: "OK in half an hour you developed the swelling yeah?"] Yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and it's getting bigger and bigger?"] Yeah it's it it  still feel it like, really pumping blood and it's kinda getting bigger it feels like it's getting worse.

[Clinician asked: "OK. So, just a few more questions. Um, so you mentioned about your swelling of your lips. Is there any feeling of swelling in in your throat, like your throat is closing up, any feeling like that?"] Yeah actually, I, I feel like I can't breathe that well right now.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And do you feel like, like your chest is tight?"] And I don't know if it's the chest it just generally feels a little difficult, but yeah it might be, I'm not sure.

[Clinician asked: "OK. OK, right. And just a few  have you ever these  any kind of lip swelling in the past? After eating any food?"] Yeah, well, maybe, I remember like a year ago. A year ago I was, out with my friends.
[Clinician asked: "Mm-hmm."] And I think we went like, to a late night kinda Chinese place, and I remember that I was quite drunk to be honest.

[Clinician asked: "Sure, OK."] And I remember that after like having that very late. I've woke up the next morning quite swollen up and had also I, felt really bad and quite difficult breathing, but I don't know what it was I had it was like a curry or something I think.

[Clinician asked: "OK. Right. OK. Uh, and what did you take at that time? Did you use anything like antihistamine or anything, when you had that last time?"] But I generally have asthma so, I took I, I take, ohh what's it called? Salbutamol inhalers?
[Clinician asked: "Yeah."] Yeah, so I do take that regularly but I've always taken it. I generally have asthma.
[Clinician asked: "And any other inhalers? Is it just the Salbutamol that you use? Any other brown inhalers?"] Ohh I, no I don't think so, no.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK right and um so, do you use antihistamines like  Cetirizine, Loratadine, Piriton, any of those, kind of things?"] Hmm, no. I, I got no I do sometimes but I don't know which ones, but I have a peanut allergy as well, like always had it.
[Clinician asked: "Yeah. Yeah."] I, think I do get antihistamines for that but I'm not sure which ones.

[Clinician asked: "And any  point because you had peanut allergy, any time you have to ever use like an injection?"] When I was a kid I think I remember my mom told me about it, but I don't know what it was 'cause I was small and I've changed doctors since.

[Clinician asked: "Right, so has your mum ever said that you had to go to hospital because you had an severe allergic reaction?"] Yeah is that well I was like six or something I, I, I think. Ohh sorry, the swelling is getting, ohh my god ohh, yeah I think when I was six.
[Clinician asked: "Oh. You all right? Are you OK now?"] Ohh, I feel a little dizzy right now to be honest, ohh.
[Clinician asked: "Are you . You're feeling dizzy. OK, right do you have any kind of, uh, antihistamines with you at the moment?"] No, I don't think I have no.
[Clinician asked: "OK, right OK, so."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>

[Clinician asked: "Right, uh, all right I, I'm really a bit worried about you at the moment, Maria.  Um I think we need to call an ambulance, because, um, from what history you've been telling me. It sounds like you could be going into what we say an a, an anaphylactic reaction. That's when your throat and your chest,"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> I'm alone at the moment, yeah.

[Clinician asked: "Ohh no so hang on the line, while I'll call the ambulance OK, and I'll send them right to you, OK and <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>."] Hey ohh somebody just came, somebody just came yes, I'm not alone any more. Thank.

[Clinician asked: "All right, OK somebody just came. So just call the  ambulance make sure that person's with you till the ambulance comes, yeah. Uh and  because we are worried about an anaphylactic reaction so you need go straight away. Could you ask the person whether they have any antihistamines around, in the inte"] Yes, uh Mum, Mum do we have any antihistamines around? Yes.

[Clinician asked: "Yes K. Do that straight away and if mum can call. Mum, if you can call the  uh nine nine , nine nine nine yeah, and say that your there is a suspected anaphylactic reaction and they need to come straight away. OK."] Ohh yeah yeah we do . Yes she said she will yes, yes.
[Clinician asked: "All right, so straight away take the antihistamines in the meantime all right."] OK, OK. Yes, thank .
[Clinician asked: "And  after you come out from the hospital  do give us a ring back we might need to sort you out, um, with your injections and things like that OK?"] Yes, yes will do, yes, thank  .
[Clinician asked: "All . OK then, take care."] Thank you very much thank bye bye.
gold_note:
History: you are having lip swelling for the past 1 hr
It started after eating a prawn soup
You initially had some weird feeling around your lips 
The swelling started after half an hour 
Now you are complaining of your throat closing 
Your chest is tight at the moment with difficulty breathing 
Examination : during the consultation, it looked like you were having some problems breathing and your lips appeared to be increasingly swelling
Past medical history : you suffer from asthma 
You have had an injection as a child for a peanut allergy 
Medication: you are currently on salbutamol inhalers 
Treatment: your Mom was around and I have advised that you use an antihistamine straight away. I have advised Mom to call the ambulance as a 999 call as we are suspecting an anaphylactic reaction
After discharge from hospital please book a review with the doctor to consider prescribing an epipen

gold_sections.PRESENTING_COMPLAINT: lips swelling after eating a sandwich
gold_sections.HIGHLIGHTS:
lip swelling
asthma
peanut allergy
anaphylactic reaction

naive.response_text: Maria, thank you for sharing your concerns with me. I understand how frightening this must be, and I'm here to support you. It sounds like you're experiencing a severe allergic reaction, which can be very serious. The ambulance is on its way, and taking the antihistamines will help manage your symptoms while you wait for help. Please know that you're not alone, and the medical team will take care of you. After you're stable, we'll work together to ensure you have the tools to manage your allergies better in the future. Stay strong, and take a deep breath—I'm thinking of you.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Maria, I can understand how alarming this must feel for you. Experiencing swelling in your lip and difficulty breathing is understandably worrying, especially after a meal. It’s important to take this seriously, as the symptoms you’re describing—swelling, difficulty breathing, and dizziness—could indicate an anaphylactic reaction, which requires immediate attention.

Given your history of similar reactions and the current severity of symptoms, it’s crucial to act quickly. Please take any antihistamines available if you can, and contact emergency services right away. Your friend’s presence is reassuring, and they should stay with you until help arrives.

Once the immediate situation is under control, we’ll need to discuss long-term management, such as obtaining an EpiPen for future use. After you’ve been evaluated, we can create a plan to prevent such reactions and ensure you’re equipped to handle any future episodes safely.

Please let me know if you need any further assistance before the ambulance arrives. We’ll follow up once you’re stable to ensure you have all the necessary tools for your health and safety.
